worth_it_explanation: Publisher Provided Identifiers (PPIDs) and first-party audience signals are how curated demand finds the right impression once third-party cookies are gone, so they decide whether a publisher's audience stays addressable to buyers who pay for it. A signal that is missing leaves curated and deal-based demand bidding blind, and a signal that is passed beyond the consent the user granted turns an addressability gain into a compliance exposure. The check reads the identity and consent wiring a publisher already has on OpenX and in the header-bidding wrapper, so it costs review time rather than rebuild time, and each finding points to a concrete change in how the identifier or the consent gate is configured.
intro: PPIDs and first-party audience signals let buyers on OpenX target a publisher's audience without a third-party cookie, which is the addressability path most curated and deal-based demand now depends on. This subcategory is about whether those identity and audience signals are passed into the bid stream at all, and whether what gets passed stays inside the consent the user actually granted. These checks confirm that the identifier reaches OpenX and its curated demand, and that the audience data attached to it is scoped to the legal basis behind it rather than sent whether or not the user agreed.

audit_checks.title: Publisher Provided IDs and first-party audience signals are not passed to OpenX
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without a Publisher Provided Identifier (PPID) or first-party audience signal in the bid stream, curated and deal-based demand on OpenX cannot target the publisher's audience once third-party cookies are absent. The impression still sells into the open auction, but at the lower, untargeted price, so the publisher keeps fill while leaving the addressability premium that curated demand pays for uncollected.

educate.base:
A PPID is a first-party user ID a publisher generates and controls, rather than a third-party cookie set by an ad tech vendor. It lets buyers recognize and target the publisher's audience across requests without relying on the cookie that browsers have removed or restricted. A first-party audience signal is the segment data a publisher attaches to that identifier, e.g., interest categories or membership in a curated audience, which is what curated deals and private marketplaces on OpenX bid against.

OpenX runs a Prebid-compatible header-bidding wrapper, so on most publisher setups the identifier travels through the Prebid User ID layer. The Publisher Provided ID module (pubProvidedId) carries the publisher's own ID into the bid request as an extended-identifier (eids) entry, and Prebid normalizes it into the ORTB eids format that bid adapters read. When that wiring is in place, OpenX and the buyers transacting through it receive the identifier and can match it against the audiences they have agreed to buy. When it is absent, the request reaches OpenX with no first-party identity attached, and curated demand has nothing to target.

The gap usually shows up in one of two ways. The publisher has built first-party audiences but never wired the identifier into the wrapper, so the segments exist in a data platform but never reach the auction. Or the identifier is passed but no audience data is attached to it, so buyers can recognize the user yet cannot tell which segment the user belongs to. Either way the curated demand that pays the addressability premium bids blind or not at all.

A few signs point to the identity and audience signal not reaching OpenX:
  • The Prebid User ID configuration includes no Publisher Provided ID entry, so no first-party identifier is added to the bid request
  • First-party audiences exist in the publisher's data platform but no path connects them to the wrapper or to OpenX
  • Curated deals or private marketplaces on OpenX report little or no matched, targetable supply from this publisher
You may want to confirm that a first-party identifier reaches OpenX and that audience data is attached to it. One approach is to inspect a live bid request for the eids entry carrying the publisher's ID, then trace whether the segment data buyers target on is connected to that identifier.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, registered readers, newsletter subscribers, and logged-in members are the strongest first-party audiences a publisher holds, and they are exactly what curated demand pays a premium to reach. You may want to confirm the identifier tied to those known users reaches OpenX with its segment data attached, so the audience the publisher has built is addressable to buyers rather than stranded in a data platform.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, audiences such as past purchasers, cart abandoners, or category browsers carry clear value to advertisers, and a first-party identifier is how that value reaches curated demand without a third-party cookie. You may want to confirm the identifier carrying those commercial segments is passed into the bid stream so OpenX buyers can target the higher-intent audience the site already knows.

learn_more.source_title: User ID Module for Header Bidding

investigate.intro: Review whether a Publisher Provided Identifier (PPID) reaches OpenX through the header-bidding wrapper, and whether first-party audience data is attached to it.

browser_devtools.label: Browser DevTools
steps: Load a page that serves OpenX demand and open the browser's developer tools to the 'Network' tab.
Filter for the bid request and inspect its payload for an 'eids' entry carrying the publisher's own first-party identifier, e.g., a source the publisher controls rather than a third-party vendor.
Note whether the identifier is present and whether any segment or audience data travels alongside it, or whether the request reaches OpenX with no first-party identity attached.
Record the source value and the identifier type so the finding names exactly what does or does not reach OpenX.

prebid_config.label: Prebid configuration review
steps: In the page's Prebid configuration, locate the User ID module setup and check whether a Publisher Provided ID entry ('pubProvidedId') is configured.
Confirm the entry supplies the publisher's first-party identifier and that the segment data buyers target on is connected to it.
Ask the publisher which first-party audiences exist in their data platform and trace whether a path connects each one to the wrapper and on to OpenX.
Record any audience that exists but never reaches the bid request. That is addressable demand the publisher is not collecting on.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a first-party Publisher Provided Identifier reaches OpenX through the wrapper, and whether audience or segment data is attached to it. A setup where the identifier is present in the bid request and connected to the publisher's first-party audiences passes. A setup where no first-party identifier reaches OpenX, or where the identifier is passed with no audience data attached while curated demand depends on it, is a fail recorded with the identifier and the audiences affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a first-party identifier reaches OpenX and whether audience data is attached to it above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not pass a first-party identifier or audience signal into the bid stream that reaches OpenX, so curated and deal-based demand cannot target the site's audience once third-party cookies are absent. The impressions still sell into the open auction, but at the untargeted price, which leaves the addressability premium that curated demand pays uncollected. {{voice_recommend}} wiring a Publisher Provided Identifier into the header-bidding wrapper through the Prebid User ID layer and connecting the site's first-party audiences to it, so OpenX buyers receive both the identity and the segment data they target on. After the change, inspect a live bid request to confirm the identifier and its audience data reach OpenX.

audit_checks.title: Audience data passed to OpenX exceeds the consent the user granted
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Passing a Publisher Provided Identifier (PPID) or audience segments without checking that the user granted the matching legal basis sends personal data into the bid stream on a basis the user never agreed to. This is a privacy and legal exposure under frameworks such as the Transparency and Consent Framework (TCF) and US state signals carried in the Global Privacy Platform (GPP), not only a revenue question, so the consequence can reach regulatory action rather than missed yield.

educate.base:
A first-party identifier and the audience data attached to it are personal data, so the legal basis to pass them into the bid stream depends on what the user consented to. The signal that records that consent is the TCF string under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), and the broader GPP string that carries TCF alongside US state signals such as those from California, Virginia, and Colorado. Whether a PPID and its segments may be sent at all is a function of those signals, not a setting the publisher chooses independently of them.

OpenX runs a Prebid-compatible wrapper, and Prebid's User ID layer is built to gate identity on consent. Its documentation states that bid adapters receive the identifier values after consent is validated, and that when the consent string withholds permission for local storage, the calls that read or set the identifier are not made. That gate only protects the user when the consent signal is wired to the identity layer correctly. If the identifier or its audience data is passed before the signal is read, or read against the wrong purpose, or the signal is missing entirely, the data flows on a basis the user never granted.

The audit question is whether what reaches OpenX stays inside the consent that backs it. Two failure shapes recur. The identifier and segments are passed regardless of the consent string, so users who declined still have their identity and audience data sent. Or the consent gate checks a purpose that does not match the use, e.g., reading a storage-access purpose while the audience data also requires a profiling or data-sharing purpose the user did not grant. In both cases the addressability gain comes at the cost of a compliance exposure.

A few signs point to audience data exceeding the consent granted:
  • The Publisher Provided ID and its segments are added to the bid request before the consent string is read, or with no check against it
  • The consent gate validates a single storage purpose while the audience data also depends on a profiling or sharing purpose that is not checked
  • Requests from users who declined consent, or from jurisdictions where a GPP signal restricts the use, still carry the identifier and audience data
It may be worth confirming that no identifier or audience data leaves the page on a basis the user did not grant. One approach is to compare a request from a user who consented against one from a user who declined, and verify the declined request carries neither the identifier nor the segments.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, the registered and subscriber audiences that make first-party data valuable are also the records most clearly tied to an identifiable person, so the consent basis behind them carries weight. You may want to confirm that a reader who declined consent has neither their identifier nor their segment data passed to OpenX, so the audience the site monetizes stays inside the permission each reader actually gave.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, purchase history and browsing segments are sensitive first-party data, and the legal basis for sharing them with buyers can differ from the basis for using them on the site itself. You may want to confirm the consent gate checks the purpose that matches sharing the data with OpenX demand, not only a storage purpose, so commercial audience data is not passed on a basis the shopper did not grant.

learn_more.source_title: User ID Module for Header Bidding

investigate.intro: Review whether the identifier and audience data passed to OpenX are gated on the consent signal, and whether the purpose checked matches how the data is used.

browser_devtools.label: Browser DevTools
steps: Load a page serving OpenX demand, grant consent through the consent management platform, and inspect the bid request for the first-party identifier and any audience segments.
Reload with consent declined, or with a Global Privacy Platform (GPP) signal that restricts the use, and inspect the same request to see whether the identifier and segments are still present.
Record any case where declined consent still produces an identifier or audience data in the request. That is data passed on a basis the user did not grant.
Check the consent string in the request against the segments carried alongside it to see whether the basis matches the data.

prebid_config.label: Prebid configuration review
steps: In the Prebid setup, confirm the consent management module is configured and that the User ID layer reads the consent string before the identifier is added to the request.
Identify which consent purpose the identity gate checks, and compare it against the purposes the audience use actually requires, e.g., profiling or data sharing, not only storage access.
Ask the publisher how the consent signal flows to OpenX and to its curated demand, and record whether the basis is carried through or dropped along the way.
Note any audience or segment that is passed without a consent check matching its use.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the identifier and audience data are gated on the consent signal and whether the purpose checked matches the use. A setup where a declined user carries neither identifier nor segments, and where the consent gate validates the purposes the data actually requires, passes. A setup where the identifier or audience data is passed regardless of consent, or gated on a purpose that does not match the use, is a fail recorded with the data and the consent basis that diverge.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether the identifier and audience data are gated on consent, and whether the purpose matches the use, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} passes a first-party identifier or audience data to OpenX on a basis wider than the consent the user granted, e.g., sending the identifier when consent was declined or gating it on a purpose that does not match the use. Because the identifier and its segments are personal data, this is a privacy and legal exposure under the Transparency and Consent Framework (TCF) and the Global Privacy Platform (GPP), not only a yield question. {{voice_recommend}} gating the identifier and audience data on the consent signal before they enter the bid request, and matching the purpose checked to how the data is used, so a declined user carries neither the identifier nor the segments. After the change, compare a consented request against a declined request to confirm the declined one passes no identity or audience data.
